"Look, the trees
are turning
their own bodies
into pillars

of light,
are giving off the rich
fragrance of cinnamon
and fulfillment,

the long tapers
of cattails
are bursting and floating away over
the blue shoulders

of the ponds,
and every pond,
no matter what its
name is, is

nameless now.
Every year
everything
I have ever learned

in my lifetime
leads back to this: the fires
and the black river of loss
whose other side

is salvation,
whose meaning
none of us will ever know.
To live in this world

you must be able
to do three things:
to love what is mortal;
to hold it

against your bones knowing
your own life depends on it;
and, when the time comes to let it
go,
to let it go."

-Mary Oliver

Long day? ☀️
 
June 21 is the summer solstice for folks north of the Equator. This date is when the Northern Hemisphere reaches its maximum tilt toward the Sun and has its longest stretch of daylight all year.
 
In the Southern Hemisphere, the opposite is true! June 21 is the start of winter and the shortest day for friends south of the Equator.
 
#NASA #Solstice #Summer

26.2 miles ✅ – Perseverance has completed the ultimate off-trail, off-world marathon!

The rover surpassed a total distance of 26.2 miles (42.195 kilometers) on the Red Planet on June 14, 2026. It crossed the milestone while exploring intriguing, ancient terrain to the west of Jezero Crater, where the robotic geologist discovered the remnants of an ancient lake, and possible signs of ancient life.

Perseverance is only the second explorer to travel the distance of a marathon on another world, following NASA’s Opportunity rover, which accomplished the feat in 2015.

#NASA #Mars #marathon

Aboard the International Space Station are a handful of small lab facilities called gloveboxes.

These are enclosed spaces that allow the astronauts on the @ISS to complete experiments with materials that need to be contained, like bacteria or reactive chemicals. Microgravity can cause these potentially hazardous materials to float—that’s where the gloveboxes come in handy!

There are two primary gloveboxes in use in the orbiting lab—the Microgravity Science Glovebox (MSG) and Life Science Glovebox (LSG)—with other portable ones that can be set up when needed. Swipe to see the main gloveboxes in action:

1. The Life Science Glovebox is, as the name suggests, ideal for biology experiments. It is built in a way to ensure no biological material gets in or out—which is necessary when working with cells and living tissue.

2. The Microgravity Science Glovebox is a space meant to mimic working laboratory conditions on Earth. It keeps the crew safe when working on experiments that include biological substances, fluids, fine particles, and even fire. The MSG was installed in 2002 and, as of this month, has been running experiments for more than 100,000 hours!

#NASA #ISS #Astronaut

Cosmic sculptors in action ⭐

You’re looking at a fresh view of the young star cluster called NGC 346, home to over 2,500 newborn stars. This cluster is a center of star formation that lies 200,000 light-years away from Earth in the Small Magellanic Cloud, a satellite galaxy of our Milky Way. 

This photo by @NASAHubble combines infrared, optical, and ultraviolet data to showcase this ghostly celestial sight.

The pink gas and dark dust snaking around the cluster are the remnants of the stars’ birthplace. The massive stars within this cluster produce intense radiation and fierce stellar winds that carve and shape the nebula that surrounds them.

#NASA #Webb #Hubble #Stars

Reaching new heights 🛫

Our experimental X-59 aircraft, which is designed to reach supersonic speeds with a quiet sonic ‘thump’, reached a new altitude of 55,000 feet on Friday, June 12.

It also flew at 1.4 times the speed of sound — about 924 miles an hour. However, for these early supersonic flights, the X-59 has been accompanied by an F-15 research aircraft whose sonic booms obscure any noise the X-59 makes. Future flights will track the X-59’s shock wave and acoustic signature to ensure its quiet thump is intact.

Each flight brings us closer to the goal of our Quesst mission: helping future air travelers get where they need to go faster without disturbing those below. Follow @NASAAero for the latest updates on the X-59 and Quesst!

Credit: NASA

#NASA #Supersonic #Aviation

Meet Europa 🧊

It's the fourth-largest moon of Jupiter. Europa is about 90% the size of our own Moon, but if it suddenly appeared in Earth's orbit, it would shine five times as bright in the night sky. This is because of its reflective, icy crust, which hides a massive subterranean ocean that could hold life beyond our home world.

Our Europa Clipper mission is on its way to learn more about this mysterious moon, and should reach Jupiter in April 2030. This view of Europa was captured by our Juno spacecraft in 2022.

Abstract plant art 🎨⁣
 ⁣
Along the Vetrivier (Vet River) in South Africa, a colorful patchwork of circular and rectangular fields spreads across what is otherwise a semi-arid part of the Free State province. In this image, each color carries physical meaning, providing clues about crop types and revealing how they changed over the course of the Southern Hemisphere’s growing season.⁣
 ⁣
The NISAR (NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar) satellite captured the data for this visualization between November 2025 and March 2026, using radar to "see" vegetation’s structure instead of its color. By combining this radar data, scientists built a compact summary of seasonal agricultural activity and change.⁣
⁣
#NASA #Farming #Plants #Science

This galaxy packs a punch

You’re looking at Messier 64, or the Black Eye Galaxy. This galaxy is located 17 million light-years away in the constellation Coma Berenices.

Messier 64 is a spiral galaxy like our own Milky Way, but it has a unique internal current that sets it apart. The gas in the outer regions of the galaxy rotates in the opposite direction of its internal regions. This contrasting motion might be caused by this galaxy merging with another one over a billion years ago. Where the opposing swirls of gases collide, new stars form.
 
This photo is a combination of views from @NASAHubble and @NASAWebb.
 
#NASA #Galaxy #Webb #Hubble
3, 2, 1, BLAST OFF! Meet the Artemis II astronauts and hear them read two exciting stories about space on the newest episode of Storytime with the Second Lady. 🌖🚀

Coming soon: one of history’s most complex missions 

Tune into our Instagram Live on Tuesday, June 9, at 11:30 a.m. EDT (1530 UTC) to meet the astronauts flying aboard Artemis III, the mission that will test rendezvous and docking capabilities with commercial landers in low Earth orbit. Commercial landers are needed to bring astronauts to the lunar surface during future Artemis missions. 

Before Artemis IV heads back to the Moon, this third mission will be an important stepping stone for lunar landings during future Artemis missions. 

Credit: NASA

#Artemis #Astronaut
There is a lot going on right now on the @iss, but fortunately we are all safe and witnessed a spectacular southern aurora show yesterday thanks to a recent solar event.

Journey to the center of the galaxy cluster

This spiral galaxy, known as Messier 88 or M88, can be found 63 million light-years from Earth in the Virgo Cluster, a collection of more than 1,000 galaxies held together by gravity. 

As its galaxies swirl and mix, M88 will spend hundreds of millions of years slowly drifting toward the innermost reaches of the Virgo Cluster. Along the way, it will get pushed and pulled by other galaxies in the cluster — reshaping it forever on its cosmic journey.

#NASA #Galaxy #TravelAddict

Soon... full moon!

Our next full moon will show up in the night skies on Sunday, May 31. Because it's the second full moon we've had this month, it's also known as a Blue Moon.

If the skies are clear in your neighborhood, this may be a great chance to take your new favorite Moon photo. Whether you're using a professional camera or just your smartphone, we have a Lunar Photography Guide with tips and tricks for shooting the Moon. (For example: if you're on your phone, consider using a photo timer to keep from jostling your phone when you snap the picture.)

Check out the full guide in our Instagram bio. 

Credit: NASA

#NASA #Moon #MoonPhotography

It’s always nighttime somewhere 🌌
 
Pause your scrolling to enjoy some night sky views of Earth from above. These photos were snapped by our astronauts aboard the @ISS as they passed over the Gulf of Thailand.
 
The dots of blue and green are the lights of squid-fishing boats. The lights attract plankton and small fish, which lure squid toward the nets.
 
#NASA #ISS #Astrophotography #ChillTime

A view earned only by leaving the world that made us.

From low Earth orbit, the planet reveals itself in motion — aurora sweeping across the night side, the atmosphere glowing in thin, electric layers, and pieces of the International Space Station drifting quietly through view. In this timelapse, astronauts capture the rhythm of Earth as only orbit can show it: fast, fluid, and alive.

Every frame is a reminder of why we study our home planet from above — to understand its systems, protect its future, and witness the beauty that unfolds just beyond the horizon.
